caveat: All figures are contested, lag events on the ground, and mix belligerent claims with independent estimates. Treat every number as an order of magnitude, not a fact.
background: The 2026 Iran war began on 28 February when the United States and Israel launched Operation Epic Fury — coordinated strikes that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in the opening hours. Iran retaliated against Israel, US bases, and Gulf states; Hezbollah opened a Lebanon front days later. A memorandum signed 17 June at Versailles and Tehran collapsed on 8 July, and fighting has since re-centered on control of the Strait of Hormuz, which the IRGC declared closed on 12 July.

status.headline: Houthis attack Saudi tankers as war widens to a second sea; House votes to fund the fight
status.detail: The war opened a second oil chokepoint in earnest and acquired a paymaster. Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis claimed attacks on two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea — the first strikes there since they declared a 'maritime embargo' on Saudi Arabia, turning a threatened blockade kinetic and layering a second contested waterway atop Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz. In Washington, the House passed a roughly $95bn Republican budget to fund the Iran war plus a $1.15tn military bill expanding cooperation with Israel, largely along party lines — the lower chamber's answer to Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's request for nearly $88bn more (about $67bn to restock munitions) atop a war he put at $37.5bn; both measures now face an uncertain Senate, where Michigan Democrat Gary Peters had told him 'you, sir, are the failure.' The US pressed a twelfth consecutive night of strikes on Iran, which Al Jazeera reported killed two people, with explosions in Sirik, Ahvaz and Bushehr, and separately signed a civil-nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia. Iran warned of an 'eye for an eye' response if Trump acts on his threat to destroy Iranian bridges and power plants for each Hormuz attack; his vow to hit the buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang) nuclear complex near Natanz 'very heavily' remains live, with IAEA safeguards suspended. Trump attended the dignified transfer of fallen US troops even as, per ABC News, he appeared to downplay the deaths online; the confirmed US toll stands at 17 dead and nearly 100 wounded.

stats.label: Killed in Iran (Iranian figures)
stats.note: US/Israeli estimates put Iranian military deaths alone above 6,000

stats.label: US service members killed
stats.note: Per an ABC News/Time tally in a war fought largely in the air; includes those killed at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase — where the WSJ reports the Iranian missile struck US personnel housing — and Sgt Michael Swinton of Fayetteville, NC, killed in Iraq during a 'controlled detonation' of a downed Iranian drone

stats.label: US service members wounded
stats.note: The Pentagon admitted nearly 100 US troops have been wounded over two weeks of escalating strikes, 21 July — a day after the New York Times reported dozens of injuries had been withheld

stats.label: Left without water in southern Iran
stats.note: Iranian figure after US strikes on water infrastructure, 18 July

stats.label: Iranian naval vessels destroyed or damaged
Direct US war cost so far
stats.note: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's figure to the Senate on 21 July; he requested roughly $88bn more (about $67bn to restock depleted munitions). On 23 July the House passed a ~$95bn budget to fund the war and a $1.15tn military bill, largely along party lines, both facing an uncertain Senate. A separate running estimate put the broader cost to US taxpayers above $113bn as of June 2026

stats.label: Global oil supply disruption
stats.note: Hormuz carries roughly a fifth of global oil flows

stats.label: Crude oil price
stats.note: Held above $90 a barrel — its highest of the war; Brent for September delivery rose ~2.7% in early Asian trade on 21 July after the tenth night of strikes and a fresh tanker fire in Hormuz

stats.label: US gasoline price
stats.note: US average gasoline topped $4 a gallon on 20 July (per ABC News); diesel has passed $5 — the war's cost reaching American drivers

stats.label: Strait of Hormuz status
stats.note: Declared closed by the IRGC on 12 July; now a live shooting zone — US striking to force it open, Iran attacking tankers to keep it shut. On 22 July Trump vowed to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for each Iranian attack in the strait; an Iranian official warned Tehran would answer by blacking out US-host Gulf states

stats.label: Houthi Red Sea blockade
stats.note: Declared against Saudi Arabia 20 July and extended to Bab el-Mandeb by 22 July; on 23 July the Houthis claimed attacks on two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea — the first strikes there since the embargo, turning the blockade kinetic. Trump vowed to 'take care of' the group — a second maritime front alongside Hormuz

stats.label: IAEA safeguards access
stats.note: Iran declared normal safeguards 'legally untenable' after strikes on Natanz; on 18 July it also suspended its commitments to the interim deal with the US

stats.label: 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang)
stats.note: On 22 July Trump threatened to hit this underground complex near Natanz 'very heavily'; it is estimated to be buried almost 100 metres inside a mountain, and little is publicly known about it

stats.label: Bushehr nuclear power plant
stats.note: Satellite images show fresh damage from strikes dated 7–12 July; Bushehr is Iran's largest nuclear plant

actors.members: United States, Israel; Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait and Bahrain after Iranian strikes; support from UK, Jordan, Qatar, Turkey and others
Iran (Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, President Pezeshkian), Hezbollah, Houthis, Popular Mobilization Forces, Hamas, Palestinian Islamic Jihad
